description: Seven-category constraint codebook for entrepreneurial discourse analysis. Categories are treated as coordinate axes in a compositional field rather than as discrete types.

financial.definition: Constraints involving money, funding, capital, cost, investment, credit, or affordability.
prototype_sentences: We didn't have the capital to scale.
The banks wouldn't lend to us.
It was too expensive to build the first version.
We couldn't afford to keep operating.

market.definition: Constraints involving customer demand, competition, market size, adoption, or sales traction.
prototype_sentences: The market just wasn't ready for our product.
We couldn't get any customers.
Everyone was doing the same thing so competition was brutal.
There was no demand for what we were building.
representative_phrases: there was no demand for product

institutional.definition: Constraints from regulation, law, policy, compliance, licensing, government, or bureaucracy.
prototype_sentences: The regulations made it nearly impossible to launch.
We had to wait months for FDA approval.
Compliance costs were enormous for a small company.
The licensing process took a year.

capability.definition: Constraints involving lack of internal knowledge, expertise, technical skill, or learning.
prototype_sentences: We didn't know how to build the tech ourselves.
The learning curve was incredibly steep.
None of us had the technical expertise.
We had to learn as we went.

talent.definition: Constraints about hiring, recruiting, retaining, or finding qualified people.
prototype_sentences: Finding senior engineers was almost impossible.
We struggled to recruit qualified people.
There was a real talent shortage in our field.
Retention was our biggest challenge.

network.definition: Constraints about industry connections, partnerships, mentors, or ecosystem access.
prototype_sentences: We had no connections in the industry.
There was no ecosystem to plug into.
We didn't know anyone in the space.
Finding a co-founder in our network was impossible.

self_imposed.definition: Voluntary constraints adopted on the basis of values, ethics, mission, or principles.
prototype_sentences: We chose not to accept certain kinds of investors.
It was against our values to expand that way.
We deliberately kept the company local.
We wouldn't compromise on our mission.

exclusion_rules.rule: Statements of the form 'we could not afford to hire X' or 'no money to hire' are coded as financial rather than talent.
exclusion_rules.rationale: The binding constraint is capital availability, not labor market conditions.

exclusion_rules.rule: Statements of the form 'we did not know how to do X' or 'had to teach ourselves' are coded as capability rather than talent.
exclusion_rules.rationale: The binding constraint is internal knowledge, not recruitment.

exclusion_rules.rule: Statements of the form 'we chose not to do X for ethical reasons' or 'against our values' are coded as self_imposed regardless of surface category.
exclusion_rules.rationale: The constraint is voluntarily adopted, not externally imposed.

excluded_patterns: this episode is brought to you by

gate_prototypes_for_embedding_fallback: We just couldn't find the money to build it.
The regulations made it nearly impossible to launch.
We had no idea how to write the software.
Finding senior engineers was almost impossible.
We had no connections in the industry.
The market just wasn't ready for our product.
We deliberately kept the company small.
It was too expensive to hire anyone.
We didn't have the technical skills.
There was no demand for the product.
The talent pool was extremely limited.
We couldn't get any investors interested.
Nobody in our network knew this space.
